chunk_summary: This section outlines follow-up protocols, referral thresholds, and metformin use for managing dysglycemia and insulin resistance in children, emphasizing risk-based cadence, numeric glycemic thresholds, and pharmacotherapy guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand follow-up cadence based on risk category and glycemic trajectory in children.
Identify referral thresholds for pediatric endocrinology in cases of diabetes-range glycemia or worsening prediabetes.
Learn metformin initiation and titration protocols for youth-onset type 2 diabetes.
Recognize the importance of distinguishing type 1 versus type 2 diabetes in pediatric cases.
key_facts: Repeat testing within 3 years for normal screening with persistent obesity/risk factors; sooner if BMI or waist trajectory worsens.
High-normal fasting glucose (95–99 mg/dL) or HbA1c (5.5–5.6%) warrants follow-up within 6–12 months during puberty.
Prediabetes requires annual reassessment, or 6-month follow-up if puberty, weight gain, or abnormal domains (BP, ALT, triglycerides) are present.
Refer to pediatric endocrinology for diabetes-range glycemia (fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL, 2-hour OGTT ≥200 mg/dL, HbA1c ≥6.5%).
Metformin initiation: Start at 500 mg daily, titrate to 2,000 mg/day as tolerated; basal insulin (0.25–0.5 units/kg/day) for HbA1c ≥8.5% without acidosis.
reference_quote: Metformin is first-line pharmacotherapy for youth-onset type 2 diabetes in most guidance, but its use 'for prediabetes' is not routine and should be specialist-linked.
document_key_topics: Follow-up cadence for dysglycemia in children
Metformin use and titration in youth-onset type 2 diabetes
Risk-based management of insulin resistance and prediabetes
